response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "Dealing with fake content online is a real headache, especially when you're constantly scrolling through your feeds. As someone who spends several hours daily on social media, I've seen my share of misinformation and outright lies being spread like wildfire. It's frustrating because sometimes it feels like half the stuff you see isn't even true. My personality might not help much—my high neuroticism means I get easily stressed by all this uncertainty. But reading novels helps me escape into something more real and less confusing, at least temporarily. However, as a native-born American with moderate conscientiousness from my upbringing (with one parent holding a master's degree), I know that critical thinking is key here.\n\nFirst off, you have to question everything. Like when I was working as a waiter/waitress in Philadelphia, we're trained to observe our customers' needs carefully without assuming too much—so applying that skepticism can be useful. Don't just believe what someone posts; dig deeper if it seems important or controversial. My parents both educated me on how facts work—I remember my mom always emphasizing double-checking things before sharing her opinion from grad school, and dad would say similar stuff about research methods while earning his bachelor's degree.\n\nAnother thing is to rely on trusted sources. I've noticed that official accounts are often more reliable than random users with no credentials. And since we're talking fake news specifically—waiters don't spread rumors in the restaurant usually because it could affect our tips and customer satisfaction—but sometimes even us foodies get tempted by viral stories about new restaurants or trends.\n\nI think staying calm is essential; my low extraversion and agreeableness make me prefer to listen rather than interrupt, so I try not to react emotionally when something suspicious pops up. It's like taking a deep breath before addressing customers with sensitive orders—being composed helps you evaluate the situation better.",

The high neuroticism level suggests that managing emotional stress through methods like reading novels can help maintain rationality while confronting misinformation.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
Dealing with fake content online is a real headache, especially when you're constantly scrolling through your feeds. As someone who spends several hours daily on social media, I've seen my share of misinformation and outright lies being spread like wildfire. It's frustrating because sometimes it feels like half the stuff you see isn't even true. My personality might not help much—my high neuroticism means I get easily stressed by all this uncertainty. But reading novels helps me escape into something more real and less confusing, at least temporarily. However, as a native-born American with moderate conscientiousness from my upbringing (with one parent holding a master's degree), I know that critical thinking is key here.

First off, you have to question everything. Like when I was working as a waiter/waitress in Philadelphia, we're trained to observe our customers' needs carefully without assuming too much—so applying that skepticism can be useful. Don't just believe what someone posts; dig deeper if it seems important or controversial. My parents both educated me on how facts work—I remember my mom always emphasizing double-checking things before sharing her opinion from grad school, and dad would say similar stuff about research methods while earning his bachelor's degree.

Another thing is to rely on trusted sources. I've noticed that official accounts are often more reliable than random users with no credentials. And since we're talking fake news specifically—waiters don't spread rumors in the restaurant usually because it could affect our tips and customer satisfaction—but sometimes even us foodies get tempted by viral stories about new restaurants or trends.

I think staying calm is essential; my low extraversion and agreeableness make me prefer to listen rather than interrupt, so I try not to react emotionally when something suspicious pops up. It's like taking a deep breath before addressing customers with sensitive orders—being composed helps you evaluate the situation better.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Based on personal experience as someone spending hours online daily and having moderate conscientiousness from parental influence (one parent has master's degree, other bachelor's), I derive that questioning information is crucial for dealing with fake content. The high neuroticism level suggests that managing emotional stress through methods like reading novels can help maintain rationality while confronting misinformation.
response_parsed.tone: Relatable and slightly stressed due to high neuroticism but tempered by moderate conscientiousness.
